text: The axial plane reveals the right renal mass, which is also visible in the coronal plane, highlighting its position and orientation within the kidney.
The video shows three bulky retroperitoneal lymph nodes, with one large lymph node prominently visible in the surgical field.
The renal vein thrombus is visible, appearing as a distinct mass within the renal vein.
The goal is to remove all disease below the diaphragm.
The surgeon incises along the white line of Toldt, mobilizing the right colon medially to expose the underlying structures.
The surgeon performs a Kocher maneuver to mobilize the duodenum, exposing the right renal hilum for further dissection.
The surgeon excises tissue over the inferior vena cava, revealing the underlying vascular structure.
The surgeon highlights the gonadal vein with a green arrow, and the ureter is also marked for identification.
The psoas muscle is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon retracts the ureter superiorly, positioning it away from the surgical field for better access to surrounding structures.
The surgeon continues the dissection along the anterior aspect of the inferior vena cava, carefully separating the surrounding tissue.
The renal vein is visible, while the renal artery is clamped and divided using a laparoscopic vascular stapler.
An ultrasound probe is utilized to delineate the boundary of the renal vein thrombus, providing a clear visual of the thrombus extent.
The thrombus is visibly extending up to the vena cava, prompting the surgeon to place a suture around the renal vein.
The surgeon uses a technique to manually advance the thrombus up the renal vein, visible as the thrombus is manipulated along the vein's length.
The surgeon places a clamp just below the suture on the renal vein, which is positioned near the inferior vena cava.
Due to limited space, the surgeon places a stick tie to secure the renal vein, ensuring control over the vessel during the procedure.
The surgeon uses both a clip and a suture to achieve venous control of the renal vein, ensuring stability and preventing blood flow during the procedure.
A second clip is placed on the renal vein, which is then opened. The surgeon carefully visualizes the lumen of the vein to confirm that it is clean and free of thrombus.
The surgeon continues dissection along the anterior surface of the inferior vena cava (IVC), dividing the overlying tissue to reach the level of the adrenal vein.
The surgeon continues dissection along the vena cava, carefully separating the adrenal gland from the vena cava using precise surgical techniques.
The surgeon clips the adrenal vein and divides it using a harmonic scalpel, ensuring clear separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon continues the superior dissection to mobilize the kidney, dividing the lateral tissue to achieve complete mobilization.
The surgeon places the kidney specimen into a fifteen millimeter endokitch bag for later retrieval. Subsequently, lymph node dissection is performed, focusing on the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon completes the mobilization of the vena cava, fully separating it from surrounding lymphatic tissue.
The surgeon highlights the renal vein on the left side, making it visually distinct from surrounding structures.
The surgeon divides the lymphatic package, focusing on the pericaval lymph nodes, using surgical instruments to separate the tissue.
The surgeon fully mobilizes a nodal packet and places it into a ten millimeter endo-catch bag for containment.
The surgeon mobilizes additional peri-cable and retro-cable lymph nodes, using clips to secure the lymphatic tissue before dividing it.
The surgeon dissects pericable lymph nodes around the renal hilum, removing bulky lymphatic tissue from the area.
Again, all visible tissue seen on the CT scan will be removed. The template for the dissection here is similar to the one used for testicular RPLND.

The surgeon mobilizes lymphatic packets and places them in a fifteen millimeter endocatch bag for later retrieval. The left renal vein is exposed, revealing its anatomical position and surrounding structures.
The surgeon exposes the aorta, which is highlighted with a green arrow, and continues to expose the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon employs a technique similar to the split and roll method used in testicular RPLND, manipulating tissue around the renal hilum.
The surgeon exposes the right renal artery within the interaerial cable space, while the inferior vena cava (IVC) is retracted superiorly to provide better access and visibility.
The surgeon highlights a bulky retrocaval lymph node and the pulsating renal artery within the interaerial cable space.
The surgeon dissects a lymph node away from the right renal artery, carefully separating the node from the artery in the surgical field.
A Kittner is used to superiorly retract the vena cava, providing access to a large bulky retrocaval lymph node. The lymph node packet is completely removed and placed into an endocatch bag.
The surgeon continues the dissection inferiorly, carefully navigating around the bifurcation of the aorta and vena cava to avoid injuring the adjacent common iliac vessels.
The surgeon dissects the inferior-most lymph node, as identified on the CT scan, carefully clipping the lymphatic tissue before cutting to prevent chyle and lymph leaks.
The surgical field shows the dissection template, including the right kidney and the left aspect of the aorta, as the surgeon prepares to continue node dissection in this area.
The surgeon uses a combination of blunt and sharp dissection techniques to carefully separate densely adhesed lymphatic tissue from the surface of the vena cava.

The surgeon examines the pericaval space, revealing the inner aorta cable space and the aorta. The right renal artery is highlighted, and the vena cava is retracted superiorly. No nodal tissues are visible.
